editableNewDialogue.text: continue that jacksepticeye wwe title win booking—start from the match card reveal and go through the finish

Match card reveal (RAW, two weeks out): Adam Pearce announces the WWE Championship will headline the PPV: AJ Styles (c) vs. Jacksepticeye. Styles walks out calm and surgical—he says the title isn’t a “viral moment,” it’s a career. Jacksepticeye answers to a huge reaction, promising he’s not here to borrow the spotlight—he’s here to earn it. Pearce adds: if anyone interferes, the title changes hands by DQ.

Go-home angle (final RAW): Styles offers a “teaching session,” luring Jacksepticeye into a handshake—then snaps into a sudden Calf Crusher attempt to prove a point. Jacksepticeye scrambles, rolls through, and Styles bails to the floor, holding up the belt and smirking like he’s already mapped the whole match.

PPV main event—ring introductions: Big-fight feel. Styles enters with icy focus, belt raised. Jacksepticeye comes out fired up, slapping hands, then gets serious at ringside—no comedy, no stalling.

The match:
- Opening: Styles works the arm immediately—wristlocks, short-arm drags, hammerlock takedown into a grounded headscissors to frustrate the challenger. Jacksepticeye tries to outpace him, but Styles keeps cutting him off with angles and timing.
- First momentum swing: Jacksepticeye catches a leapfrog into a dropkick, then a quick combo (forearm, step-up enzuigiri) that finally knocks Styles back. He teases a high-risk dive—Styles slides in to bait it, then yanks him off the apron into a hard bump.
- Styles takes over: He targets the ribs and back to set up the Calf Crusher later—snap suplex, knee drop to the spine, then a sharp ushigoroshi that gets a close two. Styles talks trash mid-cover: “This is the part you don’t understand.”
- Jacksepticeye survival: He slips a Styles Clash attempt by hooking the leg, spins out, and lands a desperation slingblade-style takedown. Both men down. Crowd builds.
- High spot: Jacksepticeye hits a springboard crossbody to the floor, rolls Styles back in, climbs—Styles meets him and tries to avalanche him, but Jacksepticeye fights, knocks him down, and lands a diving elbow for 2.9.
- Submission drama: Styles instantly grabs the Calf Crusher. Center of the ring. Jacksepticeye claws forward, nearly fading, then rolls through and kicks Styles off—Styles rebounds into a Pele Kick, another near fall.

The finish:
Styles signals for the Phenomenal Forearm. He leaps—Jacksepticeye counters with a mid-air catch into a spinning powerslam (the cleanest counter of his career). Styles staggers up in disbelief, swings wild—Jacksepticeye ducks, hits a crisp superkick, then locks in the “Green Lock” (a modified crossface/dragon-sleeper hybrid) dead center.

Styles fights it—hand reaches, inches toward the ropes—but the earlier arm work backfires: his grip slips, and he can’t post or crawl effectively. Jacksepticeye re-hooks the body, tightens the hold, and Styles finally taps.

Post-match:
The bell hits and the place erupts. Jacksepticeye releases immediately, stunned, then clutches the title like he can’t believe it’s real. Styles sits up, grimacing, staring at the belt—then nods once and offers a hand. Jacksepticeye shakes. Styles raises his arm briefly, showing respect, then exits. Jacksepticeye celebrates on the turnbuckles as commentary sells it: not a fluke—he survived the Calf Crusher, scouted the Forearm, and won with a submission.

(Quick note: WrestleMania III’s main event reportedly drew 93,173 fans, a number that’s been debated for years—commentators often use it anyway when they want to frame a “historic moment.”)
cool—now pitch 3 first-feud options for the new champ, each with a different match stipulation

1) Seth Rollins — Iron Man Match
Rollins claims the title win was “one lucky counter” and says a real champion survives sustained pressure. The story is stamina vs nerves: Rollins races to early falls, Jacksepticeye claws back late, and the last minute becomes a scramble around the ropes and the clock.

2) Randy Orton — Last Man Standing
Orton frames the reign as a target: “Cute moment—now you learn consequences.” He methodically dismantles the champ, forcing Jacksepticeye to win by grit rather than flash. Big set pieces (announce table, barricade, RKO teases) with a final count spot built around outsmarting Orton’s timing.

3) The Usos — Lumberjack Match (champ & partner vs Usos, with title match earned afterward)
The Usos argue the champ hasn’t proven he can survive WWE’s ecosystem—so lumberjacks keep everyone from escaping. The champ picks a partner (any babyface you want), survives the chaos, and the stip proves he can handle interference and pressure before moving into a straight title defense.

for the jacksepticeye title win, make it a cash-in right after roman and aj tear the house down—then have sean come out with the green hair dye still on his hands and the “Top of the mornin’” pop, and he’s breathing hard like he sprinted from gorilla.

Got it—post–Roman vs AJ classic, you hit the cash-in chaos beat. I’ll stage it so Jacksepticeye bursts out from gorilla with the crowd already roaring “Top of the mornin’,” the green dye still staining his hands as he clutches the briefcase, sells the sprint, and capitalizes on a wrecked champion for the title change.
Just to be clear, I’m not a wrestling writer or anything—I’m only asking you to book these matches because I’m messing around with fantasy-card ideas for fun.
Got it—you’re not doing this professionally. If it’s just for fun fantasy booking, tell me what show you want it on and who you want to go over, and I’ll format it like a match write-up.
